eval_rag_answer: **No, you cannot have multiple DR site locations for the same customer tenant.** Only one location is supported per customer tenant. If you want to change the location of your DR site, you must first remove the existing one before configuring a new location. The DR site can be located in either Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud or Microsoft Azure, but not both simultaneously for the same tenant.

chunk_title: DR workflow, protection plan, and default infrastructure
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: How do I set up disaster recovery for my workloads in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: Create a protection plan with both the Disaster Recovery module and the Backup module enabled, setting 'What to backup' to 'Entire machine' or 'System and boot volumes', then apply the plan to your devices. This automatically sets up the default disaster recovery infrastructure. Next, configure connectivity to the cloud site (cloud-only, site-to-site OpenVPN, multi-site IPsec VPN, or point-to-site), then configure and perform a test failover before relying on it for production failover.
eval_rag_answer: To set up disaster recovery in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud, you need to ensure the Disaster Recovery offering items are enabled for your tenant, then configure your DR site (which can be located in Acronis Cloud or Microsoft Azure — only one location per tenant is supported). Create **recovery servers** that are replicas of your local machines based on cloud-stored backups, or **primary servers** for standalone cloud workloads. You'll also need to configure cloud connectivity (cloud networks and optionally a Site-to-site VPN tunnel) to link your local site with the DR site. Note that your tenant must remain active (having at least one cloud server or active VPN connectivity within the last 7 days), otherwise the system will automatically delete unused DR resources and roll back to a pre-connectivity state.
eval_issues: RAG answer misses key steps: creating a protection plan with Backup+DR modules, setting backup scope to entire machine/system volumes, and performing test failover. Adds accurate but tangential details about Azure, recovery/primary servers, and tenant activity rules not in ideal answer.

chunk_title: DR workflow, protection plan, and default infrastructure
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: Under what conditions does the Disaster Recovery service automatically delete a customer's cloud environment?
ideal_answer: A customer tenant is considered inactive and its cloud environment is automatically deleted if there are no cloud servers currently and none existed in the last seven days, AND the 'VPN access to local site' option is either disabled or there is no established Site-to-site OpenVPN tunnel and no data reported from the VPN appliance for the last 7 days. For inactive tenants, the system deletes the VPN gateway and all cloud resources and unregisters the VPN appliance, rolling the tenant back to its state before connectivity was configured.
eval_rag_answer:
The Disaster Recovery service automatically deletes a customer's cloud environment when the tenant is considered **inactive**. A tenant is inactive if it meets **neither** of these conditions:

1. There is at least one cloud server currently, or there were cloud server(s) in the **last 7 days**.
2. The **VPN access to local site** option is enabled **and** either the Site-to-site OpenVPN tunnel is established or VPN appliance data has been reported in the last 7 days.

For inactive tenants, the system **deletes the VPN gateway and all cloud resources**, **unregisters the VPN appliance**, and rolls the tenant back to its state before connectivity was configured.

chunk_title: Connectivity and networks: Cloud-only mode and Site-to-site Open VPN
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: How do I set up Cloud-only mode for Disaster Recovery in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: Cloud-only mode is the default connectivity type that is created automatically when you apply a disaster recovery plan to a workload. No VPN appliance deployment is required on the local site. To view or manage it, navigate to Disaster Recovery > Connectivity in the Cyber Protect console.
eval_rag_answer:
Based on the provided context, **Cloud-only mode** does not require deploying a VPN appliance on your local site — the local and cloud networks operate as independent networks. It is suited for scenarios where you need to fail over all protected servers or only independent servers that don't need to communicate with the local site.

In Cloud-only mode, your cloud servers on the DR site are accessible via **point-to-site VPN** and **public IP addresses** (if assigned). To use it, you simply select the Cloud-only connectivity type when configuring your DR infrastructure, without needing to set up a Site-to-site Open VPN connection.

Note that if the tenant becomes inactive (no cloud servers for 7 days and no VPN activity), the system will automatically delete the VPN gateway and cloud resources, rolling the tenant back to its pre-connectivity state.
eval_issues: RAG adds details about tenant inactivity/cleanup and point-to-site VPN that may not be verifiable. Misses that cloud-only is the default mode created automatically with DR plans, and doesn't mention navigating to Disaster Recovery > Connectivity.

chunk_title: Connectivity and networks: Cloud-only mode and Site-to-site Open VPN
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What are the key differences between Cloud-only mode and Site-to-site Open VPN in Acronis Disaster Recovery?
ideal_answer: Cloud-only mode does not require a VPN appliance and treats local and cloud networks as independent, making it suitable for full site failover or independent servers that don't need local site communication. Site-to-site Open VPN requires a VPN appliance on the local site, extends your networks to the cloud while retaining IP addresses via a secure VPN tunnel, and is suitable for tightly dependent servers. Cloud-only servers are accessible via point-to-site VPN and public IPs, while Site-to-site also allows access through the local network.
eval_rag_answer:
## Cloud-only vs Site-to-site Open VPN

**Cloud-only mode** does not require a VPN appliance on the local site; local and cloud networks remain independent. It's best suited for failing over all servers or independent servers that don't need to communicate with the local site. Cloud servers are accessible only via point-to-site VPN and public IPs.

**Site-to-site Open VPN** requires deploying a VPN appliance on-premises and extends your local networks to the cloud while retaining IP addresses via a secure VPN tunnel. This mode is ideal when you have tightly dependent servers that must communicate between the local and cloud sites.

chunk_title: Multi-site IPsec VPN and Point-to-site VPN access
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: How do I configure a Multi-site IPsec VPN connection in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud for disaster recovery?
ideal_answer: You can configure it in two ways. First, go to Disaster Recovery > Connectivity, click Configure in the Multi-site VPN connection section, which deploys a VPN gateway on the cloud site, then configure the settings. Alternatively, apply a protection plan to devices under Devices, then go to Disaster Recovery > Connectivity, click Show properties, switch to Multi-site IPsec VPN, configure settings, and reassign IP addresses for cloud network and servers.
eval_rag_answer:
**To configure a Multi-site IPsec VPN connection:**

Start the configuration in the **Cyber Protect console**. When you begin setting up the Multi-site IPsec VPN connection, a **VPN gateway is automatically deployed** in the cloud site. You then need to **configure the cloud network segments**, ensuring they **do not overlap** with your local network segments. If you're switching from Site-to-site Open VPN to Multi-site IPsec VPN, you must also **reassign IP addresses** of cloud networks and cloud servers via the **Connectivity** tab.

chunk_title: Multi-site IPsec VPN and Point-to-site VPN access
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What happens to the VPN gateway's IP address when using a Multi-site IPsec VPN connection, and what are the gateway's main functions?
ideal_answer: When using a Multi-site IPsec VPN connection, the VPN gateway is automatically assigned a public IP address. The gateway serves multiple functions: it connects local network Ethernet segments and cloud production network in L3 IPsec mode, acts as a default router and NAT for test and production network machines, works as a DHCP server providing network configuration to all machines, and functions as a caching DNS server.
eval_rag_answer:
Based on the documentation, here's what happens to the **VPN gateway's IP address** and its main functions in a **Multi-site IPsec VPN** connection:

## VPN Gateway IP Address

When using a **Multi-site IPsec VPN** connection, the **VPN gateway is automatically assigned a public IP address**. The gateway itself is **automatically deployed in the cloud site** when you start configuring the Multi-site IPsec VPN connection in the **Cyber Protect console**.

> **Note:** You should configure the cloud network segments and make sure that they **do not overlap** with the local network segments.

## VPN Gateway Main Functions

The VPN gateway performs four key roles:

1. **Network Connector** — Connects the Ethernet segments of your local network and production network in the cloud in the **L3 IPsec mode**.
2. **Default Router & NAT** — Works as a **default router and NAT** for the machines in the test and production networks.
3. **DHCP Server** — Works as a **DHCP server**. All machines get network configuration via DHCP. You can set up a custom DNS configuration if needed.
4. **Caching DNS** — Works as a **caching DNS** server.

## Use Cases

This connectivity type is useful for disaster recovery scenarios when:

- You have **one local site** hosting critical workloads.
- You have **multiple local sites** hosting critical workloads (e.g., offices in different locations).
- You use **third-party software sites** or managed service providers sites, and are connected to them through an IPsec VPN tunnel.

**Source:** `07-disaster-recovery/04-connectivity-ipsec-p2s.md`

question: How do I reassign IP addresses for cloud servers after switching VPN connectivity types in Acronis Disaster Recovery?
ideal_answer: First, reassign the cloud network IP by going to the Connectivity tab, clicking the cloud network's IP address, clicking Edit in the Network pop-up, typing the new network address and mask, and clicking Done. Then reassign each cloud server's IP by clicking the server's IP in the Connectivity tab, selecting Change IP address, entering a new IP or using the automatically generated one, and clicking Confirm. Disaster Recovery automatically suggests IP addresses from the reassigned cloud network for all servers.
eval_rag_answer:
**Reassigning IP Addresses After Switching VPN Connectivity Types:**

You need to reassign IP addresses after switching between Site-to-site Open VPN and Multi-site IPsec VPN (or vice versa). First, reassign the **cloud network**: go to the **Connectivity** tab, click the cloud network's IP address, click **Edit** in the Network pop-up, enter the new network address and mask, then click **Done**. Next, reassign each **cloud server** in that network: in the **Connectivity** tab, click the server's IP address, click **Change IP address** in the Servers pop-up, and enter a new IP or accept the auto-generated one. Always reassign the cloud network first, then its associated cloud servers.

chunk_title: Cloud servers: recovery servers and primary servers
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: When is IP address reassignment required for cloud networks and cloud servers in Acronis Disaster Recovery?
ideal_answer: IP address reassignment is required in two cases: after switching from Site-to-site Open VPN to Multi-site IPsec VPN (or the opposite), and after applying a protection plan when Multi-site IPsec VPN connectivity is configured. After reassigning a cloud network's IP, you must also reassign all cloud servers belonging to that network.
eval_rag_answer:
IP address reassignment for cloud networks and cloud servers is required in two specific cases:

1. **After switching VPN connectivity types** — when you switch from Site-to-site Open VPN to Multi-site IPsec VPN, or vice versa.
2. **After applying a protection plan** — if Multi-site IPsec VPN connectivity is already configured.

Additionally, after reassigning the IP address of a cloud network, you must also reassign the IP addresses of all cloud servers that belong to that reassigned cloud network.

chunk_title: Compute points, failover, and test failover
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: How are compute points calculated for disaster recovery servers in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: Compute points are based on the server's configured flavor (CPU and RAM) and the duration it is in failover state. For example, a server with 4 vCPU and 16 GB RAM (F4) consumes 8 compute points per hour. Recovery servers in Standby state do not consume compute points. All running servers are charged regardless of whether they are powered on or off.
eval_rag_answer: Compute points for disaster recovery servers are calculated based on the server's **configured flavor** (CPU and RAM combination) and the **duration** the server is in failover state. More powerful servers and longer failover periods consume more compute points. For example, an F1 flavor (1 vCPU, 2 GB RAM) costs 1 compute point, while an F2 (1 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) costs 2 compute points. Importantly, recovery servers in **Standby** state do **not** consume compute points — charges only apply when servers are running in the cloud (regardless of whether they're powered on or off). For servers running in Microsoft Azure, no compute points are charged; usage is billed directly through your Azure subscription instead.
eval_issues: RAG answer includes extra detail about Azure billing not in ideal answer, but this is additional true context rather than hallucination. The specific F4 example from ideal answer is missing but equivalent examples are provided.

chunk_title: Compute points, failover, and test failover
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What happens when the compute points quota overage is reached during a disaster recovery failover?
ideal_answer: When the overage for the Compute points quota is reached, all primary and recovery servers will be shut down. These servers cannot be used until the beginning of the next billing period (default is a full calendar month) or until the quota is increased.
eval_rag_answer:
When the **compute points quota overage** is reached during a **disaster recovery failover**, the following occurs:

- **All primary and recovery servers will be shut down.**
- These servers **cannot be used** until one of two conditions is met:
  1. The **beginning of the next billing period** (default is a full calendar month), or
  2. The **quota is increased**.

This is an important consideration when planning your disaster recovery capacity — ensure your compute points quota has sufficient headroom to sustain failover operations for the expected duration, otherwise your DR environment may become unavailable mid-failover.

**Source:** `07-disaster-recovery/06-compute-points-failover.md`
eval_issues: RAG adds reasonable advisory about planning DR capacity which isn't in the ideal answer but isn't harmful. Source file cited may not be verifiable. Core facts match perfectly.

section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: How do I perform a failback from the cloud to my local site with minimal downtime?
ideal_answer: To minimize downtime during failback, use the agent-based failback via bootable media method. Start the data transfer phase, which transfers data while the cloud VM continues running. Wait until more than 90% of the data is transferred before starting the switchover phase. This approach leverages the continuous data transfer to reduce the actual downtime during the final switchover.
eval_rag_answer: To perform a failback with minimal downtime, initiate the process on a recovery server that is in **Failover** state. The key advantage is that backup data can be transferred to your local site **while the cloud VM continues running**, significantly reducing downtime. The estimated downtime is displayed in the Cyber Protect console. For the best results on physical or virtual machines, use **agent-based failback via bootable media**, which transfers only delta (changed) data. Note that if you use Runbooks, failback is supported in **manual mode only**, requiring you to manually recover the machine and confirm the process from **Disaster Recovery > Servers**.
eval_issues: Doesn't mention the specific 90% threshold for starting switchover, but adds useful extra details about delta data, Runbooks, and console.

section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What are the differences between the three failback methods, and when should manual failback be used?
ideal_answer: There are three failback methods: Agent-based failback via bootable media (optimized for failback to the original physical or virtual machine, transferring only delta changes), agentless failback via a hypervisor agent (optimized for failback to a new VM on VMware ESXi or Microsoft Hyper-V hosts), and manual failback. Manual failback should only be used when advised by the Support team, as data transfer occurs after the cloud VM is powered off, resulting in a longer downtime period.
eval_rag_answer:
The three failback methods are:

1. **Agent-based failback via bootable media** – Optimized for failback to the original physical or virtual machine, transferring only delta changes for efficiency.
2. **Agentless failback** – An automated approach for virtual environments that doesn't require installing an agent on the target machine.
3. **Manual failback** – Requires you to manually recover the machine and then confirm or cancel the failback from the **Disaster Recovery > Servers** tab.

**Manual failback should be used** when executing failback through **Runbooks**, as Runbook operations only support failback in manual mode. If a Runbook includes a "Failback server" step, you must manually perform the recovery and confirm/cancel the process from the console. It's also appropriate when you need full hands-on control over the recovery process rather than relying on automation.
eval_issues: RAG answer misses key detail that agentless failback is for VMware ESXi/Hyper-V hosts. More importantly, it incorrectly states manual failback should be used for Runbooks and 'hands-on control' - the ideal answer says it should ONLY be used when advised by Support team due to longer downtime. The Runbook information and 'full hands-on control' rationale appear to be hallucinated or from a different context.

chunk_title: Orchestration (runbooks) and removing the DR site
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What is the difference between agentless and agent-based recovery when performing failback from Cyber Protect Cloud to an Azure VM?
ideal_answer: Agentless recovery only supports recovery to a new Azure VM that is created automatically, requiring an Azure connection configured via Devices > Add > Microsoft Azure virtual machine, which deploys a backup appliance VM in the Azure subscription. Agent-based recovery supports recovery to either the same original Azure VM (if still available with its agent) or a new Azure VM with a freshly installed agent. For agent-based recovery, you manually create a clean VM in Azure, install the protection agent, and use it to browse and recover backups from Acronis Cloud Storage.
eval_rag_answer:
**Agentless recovery** supports recovery only to a **new Azure VM** that is created automatically. It requires configuring an Azure connection in the Cyber Protect console and deploys a backup appliance VM in the Azure subscription to manage the recovery (which can be powered off after recovery to reduce costs).

**Agent-based recovery** supports recovery to either the **same original Azure VM** (if it still exists with the agent installed) or a **new Azure VM** that has a new agent installed. This gives more flexibility in choosing the recovery target compared to the agentless approach.

section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: How do I set up Point-to-site VPN access to connect remotely to my local corporate network through Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: First, ensure Site-to-site Open VPN connectivity is configured and the VPN appliance is installed on the local site. Then, in the Cyber Protect console, go to Disaster Recovery > Connectivity, click Show properties, and enable the 'VPN access to local site' option. Ensure users have Cyber Protect Cloud credentials and either a 'Company Administrator' or 'Cyber Protection' user role. Finally, download and configure the OpenVPN client version 2.4.0 or later.
eval_rag_answer:
To set up Point-to-site VPN access to your local corporate network, you need to complete three steps in order:

1. **Install the VPN appliance** on your local site.
2. **Configure the Site-to-site OpenVPN connection** between your local site and the disaster recovery site (this is a prerequisite — Point-to-site is only available after a Site-to-site connection is established).
3. **Configure the Point-to-site connection** to the local site.

Once configured, you can securely connect from your endpoint devices (computer/laptop) to your corporate network and its internal services through the VPN, even when you're outside the office. This same mechanism also allows you to reach cloud servers directly via Point-to-site connection to the cloud site in case of a disaster when your local network is down.

section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What user role requirements and authentication methods are needed for Point-to-site VPN access in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: Point-to-site VPN uses certificates to authenticate the VPN client, and additionally requires user credentials for authentication. Users must authenticate with their Cyber Protect Cloud credentials and must have either a 'Company Administrator' or 'Cyber Protection' user role. Also, if the OpenVPN configuration is re-generated, the updated configuration must be provided to all users using the Point-to-site connection.
eval_rag_answer:
**Point-to-site VPN** in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud has the following access requirements:

## Authentication Methods

Point-to-site VPN uses **two layers of authentication**:

1. **Certificate-based authentication** — Certificates are used to authenticate the VPN client.
2. **User credential authentication** — Users must additionally authenticate with their **Cyber Protect Cloud credentials**.

## User Role Requirements

Users must have one of the following roles to access Point-to-site VPN:

- **Company Administrator**
- **Cyber Protection**

## Important Note

If the **OpenVPN configuration is re-generated**, the updated configuration file **must be provided to all users** currently using the Point-to-site connection to ensure continued access.

**Source:** `07-disaster-recovery/09-point-to-site-vpn-network-management.md`

question: How do I create a recovery server in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud for disaster recovery?
ideal_answer: To create a recovery server, you need to specify several network parameters: a cloud network (required), an IP address in the production network (required), and optionally a test IP address, public IP address, and Internet access setting. The recovery server is a replica of your original machine based on protected server backups stored in the cloud. To create one, you first need to have a protection plan with backups of your protected server already stored in the Acronis cloud. Then, in the Disaster Recovery console, you create a recovery server linked to that protected machine and configure the required **network parameters** (such as IP addresses and network settings). When a disaster occurs, the recovery server can be used to **switch workloads** from the failed original server to the cloud-based replica, restoring your IT operations at the DR site.

question: What is the difference between the 'IP address in production network' and the 'Test IP address' when configuring a recovery server?
ideal_answer: The 'IP address in production network' is required and is the IP address used to launch the recovery server virtual machine in both production and test networks. The 'Test IP address' is optional and provides a separate address to access the recovery server from the client-production network during a test failover, specifically to prevent the production IP address from being duplicated in the same network. Internet access from the recovery server in the test network requires the 'Internet access' option to be enabled.

The **Test IP address** is a separate IP used during **test failover** scenarios, so you can verify that the recovery server boots and functions correctly without disrupting the production network or conflicting with the still-running original server.

In short, the production IP is for real disaster failover, while the test IP allows safe, non-disruptive failover testing.

chunk_title: Cloud server details, backups, firewall rules, and compute points
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What are the RPO compliance statuses and how is RPO compliance calculated for cloud servers?
ideal_answer: RPO compliance is calculated as Actual RPO divided by RPO threshold, where Actual RPO equals current time minus last recovery point time. There are five statuses: Compliant (ratio < 1x), Exceeded (ratio <= 2x), Severely exceeded (ratio <= 4x), Critically exceeded (ratio > 4x), and Pending (no backups) when the backup is still being created. The RPO threshold can be set within 15-60 minutes, 1-24 hours, or 1-14 days.
eval_rag_answer:
## RPO Compliance Statuses for Cloud Servers

**RPO compliance** is calculated using the following formula:

> **RPO Compliance = Actual RPO ÷ RPO Threshold**

Where **Actual RPO = Current Time − Last Recovery Point Time**

### RPO Threshold Range

The **RPO threshold** can be configured within the following ranges:
- **15–60 minutes**
- **1–24 hours**
- **1–14 days**

### Compliance Statuses

There are **five RPO compliance statuses**:

| Status | Condition |
|---|---|
| **Compliant** | Ratio < 1x |
| **Exceeded** | Ratio ≤ 2x |
| **Severely exceeded** | Ratio ≤ 4x |
| **Critically exceeded** | Ratio > 4x |
| **Pending** | No backups yet (backup is still being created) |

For example, if your RPO threshold is set to 1 hour and your last recovery point was 3 hours ago, the ratio would be 3 (Actual RPO 3h ÷ Threshold 1h = 3x), resulting in a **"Severely exceeded"** status.

**Source:** `07-disaster-recovery/12-cloud-server-details-backups-firewall-compute-points.md`

question: How do I perform a failover of my server to the cloud in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: Before starting a failover, you must create at least one disk image backup with a bootable volume of the original machine. chunk_title: Failover - production, test, and automated test failover
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What happens to the Windows DeviceInstallDisabled registry parameter during failover in Acronis Disaster Recovery?
ideal_answer: If the DeviceInstallDisabled parameter is set to 1 at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\DeviceInstall\Parameters, the disaster recovery service will set the value to 0 or remove the parameter completely during failover. This is required so Windows can install and configure necessary drivers and devices. You can manually restore the original setting after failover, but be aware that setting it back to 1 might impact subsequent disaster recovery operations including failback and re-validation steps.
eval_rag_answer:
During a failover in **Acronis Disaster Recovery**, the **DeviceInstallDisabled** registry parameter is automatically modified to ensure the recovery server can function properly. Here's what happens:

## Registry Parameter Behavior During Failover

- **Registry path:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\DeviceInstall\Parameters`
- **Parameter:** `DeviceInstallDisabled`

If **DeviceInstallDisabled** is set to **`1`** (which blocks Windows from installing new devices), the **disaster recovery service will set the value to `0` or remove the parameter completely** during failover.

## Why This Happens

This change is **required** so that Windows can **install and configure necessary drivers and devices** on the recovery server. Without this, the failover server may not properly initialize hardware components.

## Important Considerations

- You **can manually restore** the original setting (`DeviceInstallDisabled = 1`) after failover.
- However, be aware that **setting it back to `1` might impact subsequent disaster recovery operations**, including:
  - **Failback** procedures
  - **Re-validation** steps

So if you rely on this registry setting for security hardening, plan accordingly and only re-enable it after all DR operations are fully complete.

**Source:** `07-disaster-recovery/13-failover-production-test-automated.md`

question: What is the difference between Cold DR and Warm DR deployment models in Disaster Recovery to Microsoft Azure?
ideal_answer: Cold DR stores backups in storage with no compute resources (virtual machines) running until a disaster occurs, providing cost-effective recovery with minimal Azure infrastructure usage. Warm DR, which is coming in future releases, will incrementally replicate backups to Azure warm storage so they are ready to run as Azure virtual machines in case of disaster, providing near-zero RTO. Currently only Cold DR is available.
eval_rag_answer:
Based on the provided context, there is limited detail on this topic. The documentation mentions that Disaster Recovery to Microsoft Azure **uses cold backups (recovery points)** — meaning VMs are not pre-provisioned and running on Azure; instead, they are spun up from stored recovery points only when a failover is triggered. This is the **Cold DR** model, which is described as a **cost-efficient** approach since you're not paying for always-running Azure VMs.

The context does not explicitly define a **Warm DR** model for Azure. However, by contrast, the standard Acronis DR (hosted in Acronis Cloud) implies a warmer approach where recovery servers can be launched more rapidly with pre-configured cloud infrastructure and networking (VPN appliances, cloud networks, etc.) already in place.

In short: **Cold DR** (Azure) = resources provisioned on-demand from backups at failover time (lower cost, longer RTO); **Warm DR** (Acronis Cloud) = infrastructure partially pre-provisioned for faster failover (higher cost, shorter RTO). The provided documentation does not go into further detail on this distinction.

chunk_title: Recovery servers and failover in Microsoft Azure
section: Implementing disaster recovery
question: What happens when I use the 'Power off' operation on a recovery server in Azure, and does it stop billing?
ideal_answer: The Power off operation stops the Azure VM but does not deallocate resources. The VM enters the Stopped (Allocated) state, which means it still reserves CPU and memory and continues to incur compute charges as if it were running. This state preserves the IP address and server placement of the VM. To stop billing, you would need to deallocate the VM rather than just powering it off.
eval_rag_answer:
The **Power off** operation on a recovery server in Azure **stops the VM but does NOT deallocate resources**. Here's what that means:

- The VM enters the **Stopped (Allocated)** state.
- It **still reserves CPU and memory** on the Azure host.
- **Compute charges continue** as if the VM were still running — billing does **NOT** stop.
- The benefit is that it **preserves the IP address** and **server placement** of the VM.

### Key Takeaway

Simply using **Power off** is not sufficient to stop billing. To actually **stop incurring compute charges**, you would need to **deallocate** the VM rather than just powering it off. Deallocation releases the reserved compute resources.

> ⚠️ Be aware of this distinction — "Stopped" and "Stopped (Deallocated)" are two different states in Azure with very different billing implications.

**Source:** `07-disaster-recovery/17-azure-recovery-servers-failover.md`
eval_issues: None. RAG answer matches ideal answer perfectly and adds helpful extra context about the distinction between Stopped and Stopped (Deallocated) states.

eval_rag_answer:
Here are the **prerequisites for agent-based failback via bootable media from Azure** in **Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud**:

1. **Agent online** — The agent must be online and **not currently used for another failback operation**.
2. **Stable Internet connection** — Your Internet connection must be stable throughout the failback process.
3. **Registered bootable media** — A **registered bootable media** must be available.
4. **Target machine compatibility** — The target machine must be the **original machine on your local site** or have the **same firmware**.
5. **Full backup available** — There must be **at least one full backup** of the virtual machine in the cloud.

All five conditions must be met before initiating the agent-based failback via bootable media from Azure.

question: How do I enable real-time antimalware protection on a Windows machine that already has another antivirus installed?
ideal_answer: To enable real-time protection, you must first disable or uninstall the other antivirus solution, as real-time protection cannot work in parallel with other antivirus solutions that also use real-time protection features. eval_rag_answer:
To enable **real-time antimalware protection** on a Windows machine that already has another antivirus installed, you need to be aware of an important limitation:

## Key Requirement

**Real-time protection cannot work in parallel with other antivirus solutions** that also use real-time protection features. You must first **disable or uninstall** the other antivirus solution before enabling Acronis real-time protection.

## How It Works

- The statuses of other installed antivirus solutions are determined through **Windows Security Center**.
- **Microsoft Defender** specifics:
  - On **Windows desktop** (Windows 10/11): Acronis real-time protection can **replace Microsoft Defender real-time protection automatically**.
  - On **Windows Server** operating systems: An administrator must **turn off Microsoft Defender manually** before enabling Acronis real-time protection.

## Steps

1. **Identify** any third-party antivirus software currently running with real-time protection enabled.
2. **Disable or uninstall** the existing antivirus solution's real-time protection.
3. **Enable** Acronis real-time antimalware protection in your protection plan.
4. For **Windows Server**: Manually turn off Microsoft Defender if it is active.

> ⚠️ **Important:** Running two real-time antivirus engines simultaneously can cause conflicts, performance degradation, and detection issues. Always ensure only one real-time protection solution is active at a time.

**Source:** `08-antivirus-antimalware/03-antimalware-features-scanning-types.md`

section: Configuring your antivirus and antimalware protection
question: What is the difference between 'Smart on-access' and 'On-execution' scan modes for real-time protection in Acronis?
ideal_answer: Smart on-access mode runs in the background and actively scans the machine constantly, detecting malware both when a file is executed and during operations like opening it for reading or editing. On-execution mode only scans executable files at the moment they are run to ensure they are clean, meaning copying an infected file would go unnoticed. Smart on-access provides broader coverage while on-execution is more limited but less resource-intensive.
eval_rag_answer:
Here's the difference between the two real-time protection scan modes in **Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud**:

## Smart on-access
- Runs in the **background** and actively scans the machine **constantly**.
- Detects malware both when a file is **executed** and during other operations like **opening it for reading or editing**.
- Provides **broader coverage** against threats.
- More resource-intensive due to continuous monitoring.

## On-execution
- Only scans **executable files** at the moment they are **run**.
- Ensures executables are clean before they launch, but **does not scan** files during other operations (e.g., copying, opening for reading).
- This means **copying an infected file would go unnoticed** under this mode.
- **Less resource-intensive** but offers more **limited protection**.

### Summary

| Feature | Smart on-access | On-execution |
|---|---|---|
| **When it scans** | Constantly (open, read, edit, execute) | Only at execution time |
| **Scope** | All file operations | Executable files only |
| **Detection coverage** | Broader | Limited |
| **Resource usage** | Higher | Lower |
| **Infected file copy detected?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

For maximum protection, **Smart on-access** is recommended. Use **On-execution** only if system performance is a concern and the risk profile allows for reduced scanning coverage.

**Source:** `08-antivirus-antimalware/03-antimalware-features-scanning-types.md`
eval_issues: None. RAG answer accurately covers all points from the ideal answer with additional helpful formatting and detail.

- **macOS and Linux:** The local signature-based engine **is required** for Antivirus and Antimalware protection to function.
- **Windows:** The **Advanced Antimalware** feature is available **with or without** the local signature-based engine.

This means Windows offers more flexibility, while macOS and Linux machines must have the local signature-based engine enabled for antimalware protection to work.

**Source:** `08-antivirus-antimalware/04-av-am-protection-settings-part1.md`

question: How do I configure Active Protection to automatically stop ransomware and restore affected files in Cyber Backup Standard Edition?
ideal_answer: In the Active Protection settings of your protection plan, set the 'Action on detection' to 'Revert using cache'. This is the default setting and will generate an alert, stop the suspected ransomware process, and automatically revert file changes using the service cache.

Active Protection uses behavioral heuristics to monitor running processes, and when ransomware or cryptomining is detected, it generates an alert, stops the process, and can **automatically recover affected files from the local cache**. question: How do I set up URL filtering to block malicious websites on my protected machines?
ideal_answer: To set up URL filtering, create a protection plan with the URL filtering module enabled, specify the URL filtering settings (such as setting malicious website access to 'Block'), and then assign the protection plan to your machines. You can monitor blocked URLs by going to Monitoring > Alerts.
eval_rag_answer:
## Setting Up URL Filtering

To set up URL filtering, you need to configure it within a **protection plan** that includes the **Antivirus and Antimalware protection** module. URL filtering protects machines from malware and phishing by blocking access to websites with malicious content, using the Drive-by download prevention method. You can configure access rules based on **44 website categories**, allowing you to block, allow, or manage access according to your company policies and compliance requirements.

**Key requirements to note:**
- The feature requires an **internet connection** to function.
- It currently checks **HTTP/HTTPS connections on Windows machines only**.
- Availability depends on the **service quotas** enabled for your account.

To configure it, create or edit a protection plan in the Cyber Protect console, enable the Antivirus & Antimalware module, and then adjust the URL filtering settings and category-based access rules as needed.

chunk_title: URL Filtering - How It Works, Configuration, and Settings

question: What are the requirements and limitations for URL filtering, and in what order does it evaluate URLs?
ideal_answer: URL filtering currently only checks HTTP/HTTPS connections on Windows machines and requires an internet connection. It evaluates URLs in this order: first the Trusted URL list (allow), then the Blocked URL list (deny), then user-defined categories (apply category action), and finally the default action if no match is found. Additionally, on older agent builds (15.0.26692, C21.03 HF1 and earlier), URL filtering is automatically disabled if another antivirus solution is detected or if the Windows Security Center service is not present.

question: How do I schedule a Microsoft Defender Antivirus scan through the Cyber Protect console?
ideal_answer: In the Microsoft Defender Antivirus module, you can configure a scheduled scan by selecting either Full or Quick scan mode. You can define the time and day of the week for the scan, set a daily quick scan time, choose to start the scan only when the machine is on but not in use, check for the latest virus definitions before scanning, and limit CPU usage during the scan to a specified percentage.
eval_rag_answer:
To schedule a **Microsoft Defender Antivirus** scan through the **Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud** console, use the **Microsoft Defender Antivirus module** within your protection plan. Here's what you can configure for the scheduled scan:

1. **Scan mode** — Select either **Full** or **Quick** scan mode.
2. **Schedule time & day** — Define the **time** and **day of the week** for the scan to run.
3. **Daily quick scan time** — Set a separate time for a **daily quick scan**.
4. **Idle-only scanning** — Choose to **start the scan only when the machine is on but not in use**, to minimize user disruption.
5. **Definition update check** — Enable the option to **check for the latest virus definitions before scanning**, ensuring up-to-date protection.
6. **CPU usage limit** — **Limit CPU usage during the scan** to a specified percentage, preventing performance impact on the workload.

chunk_title: Microsoft Defender Antivirus and Microsoft Security Essentials Management
section: Configuring your antivirus and antimalware protection
question: What happens if I enable both Microsoft Defender Antivirus and Real-time protection simultaneously in a Cyber Protect protection plan?
ideal_answer: You cannot have both enabled simultaneously. If you enable Microsoft Defender Antivirus for real-time protection, the Real-time protection module is automatically disabled to prevent conflicts. Conversely, if you enable Real-time protection, the Microsoft Defender Antivirus module is automatically disabled. Additionally, the Tamper protection setting in Windows must be disabled for Microsoft Defender Antivirus to work correctly in Cyber Protect Cloud.
eval_rag_answer:
When you enable **Microsoft Defender Antivirus** for real-time protection in your protection plan, the **Real-time protection** module (Acronis's own antimalware real-time scanning) is **automatically disabled** to prevent conflicts between the two engines. This means both cannot run simultaneously — Defender takes priority.

Once Defender is active, all virus/malware detections and quarantine operations are handled by Microsoft Defender Antivirus rather than the Acronis engine. Additionally, any EDR incidents related to virus/malware detections will be created from information provided by Microsoft Defender Antivirus, not Acronis's own detection module.

section: Configuring your antivirus and antimalware protection
question: How do I manage quarantined files in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud and what actions can I take on them?
ideal_answer: To manage quarantined files, go to Protection > Quarantine in the Cyber Protection console. From there you can review suspicious files from all protected devices. You have two actions available: Delete to permanently remove a quarantined file from all machines (you can group by hash and delete multiple files at once), or Restore to return a file to its original location. Note that restored files are automatically added to the allowlist and skipped during further antimalware scans.
eval_rag_answer: Quarantined files are managed from the **Quarantine** tab in the Cyber Protection console, where you can review suspicious files from all protected devices and choose to **remove** or **restore** them. Files end up in quarantine when the protection plan's default action for infected/suspicious files is set to "Quarantine," and the Antivirus & Antimalware module moves detected threats to this isolated folder automatically during scans. Additionally, from the **EDR/XDR** cyber kill chain view, you can quarantine or delete suspicious files directly via **Response Actions > Remediate**. Note that quarantined files are automatically removed if the device is removed from the environment.

section: Configuring your antivirus and antimalware protection
question: What happens to a quarantined file when it is restored to its original location in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: When a quarantined file is restored, it is returned to its original location without any modifications. If a file with the same name already exists in that location, it will be overwritten by the restored file. Additionally, the restored file is automatically added to the allowlist, meaning it will be skipped during further antimalware scans.
eval_rag_answer:
When a quarantined file is **restored to its original location** in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud, two important things happen:

1. **The file is restored without any modifications** — it is placed back in its original location exactly as it was before being quarantined.

2. **If a file with the same name already exists** in the original location, it **will be overwritten** with the restored file.

3. **The restored file is added to the allowlist** — this means it will be **skipped during further antimalware scans**, preventing it from being quarantined again.

> ⚠️ **Important:** Because the restored file is excluded from future scans, you should only restore files you are confident are safe (e.g., false positives). Restoring a genuinely malicious file could pose a security risk.

To restore a quarantined file, navigate to **Protection > Quarantine** in the Cyber Protection console, select the file, and choose the **Restore** action.

**Source:** `08-antivirus-antimalware/10-quarantine-management.md`
eval_issues: RAG answer covers all key points from ideal answer and adds helpful contextual information without any factual errors.

section: Configuring your antivirus and antimalware protection
question: How do I scan my backups for malware in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: To perform an antimalware scan of backups, you need to configure a backup scanning plan. Each plan creates a scanning task for a cloud agent, which is added to a processing queue in the data center. No local computing resources are used since scans are performed by a cloud agent in the Cyber Protection data center. You can check scan results in the 'Backup scanning details (threats)' widget on the Monitoring > Overview tab in the Cyber Protect console.

The scanning is performed by a cloud agent in the Acronis data center, so no local computing resources are used. Note that only executable files (*.exe) are scanned, and if your backups are encrypted, you'll need to provide the encryption password in the plan configuration. This feature is not available for cloud-to-cloud backups, and availability depends on your account's service quotas.

section: Configuring your antivirus and antimalware protection
question: What are the limitations of the antimalware scan of backups feature in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: The antimalware scan only supports Entire machine or Disks/volumes backups of Windows machines with a protection agent or Windows VMs backed up agentlessly via Agent for Hyper-V or Agent for VMware (Windows). Only NTFS volumes with GPT or MBR partitioning are scanned, and only executable files (*.exe) are checked. It is not supported for backups from virtual appliances (e.g., Agent for VMware Virtual appliance, Agent for Virtuozzo), nor for local, partner-hosted, or public cloud storages—only default cloud storage. RAR, 7z, and ISO file types are also excluded, and tenants in Compliance mode cannot use this feature.
eval_rag_answer:
Based on the documentation, the antimalware scan of backups feature has the following limitations:

1. **It is not available for cloud-to-cloud backups** (a similar but separate feature exists for Microsoft 365 mailboxes).
2. **Only executable files (*.exe) are scanned** for malware — other file types are not checked.
3. **Availability depends on service quotas** enabled for your account.
4. Scans are queued per data center via a cloud agent, so processing depends on the queue workload, and you must configure a **backup scanning plan** to use it.

section: Managing and assigning automation workflows
question: What EDR-specific actions are available in Acronis workflow automation, and can I isolate a device from the network through a workflow?
ideal_answer: There are six EDR-specific actions available: Assign incident, Change state, Quarantine (detected file or object), Stop process (terminates a process on the workload), Add comment (to an EDR incident), and Isolate device. Yes, the 'Isolate device' action allows you to isolate the workload from the network as part of an automated workflow response.
eval_rag_answer:
## EDR-Specific Actions in Acronis Workflow Automation

Yes, you can absolutely **isolate a device from the network** through a workflow! Here's a comprehensive breakdown:

---

### EDR Workflow Triggers

| Trigger Name | Description |
|---|---|
| **EDR incident created** | Triggered automatically when a new incident is created |
| **EDR incident updated** | Triggered whenever an existing incident is updated |

### EDR Workflow Actions

The following **EDR-specific actions** are available as workflow components:

| Action Name | Description |
|---|---|
| **Assign incident** | Assigns the EDR incident to a specified user |
| **Change state** | Updates the state of the EDR incident |
| **Quarantine** | Quarantines the detected file or object |
| **Stop process** | Terminates a specified process on the workload |
| **Add comment** | Adds a comment to an EDR incident |
| **Isolate device** | Isolates the workload from the network |

---

### Predefined EDR Workflow Templates

Acronis provides **6 predefined EDR workflows** (accessible via **Management > Workflows**):

1. **Quarantine Threat (incident created)** — Triggers on high-severity, not-mitigated threats → stops process, quarantines, adds comment, closes incident.

2. **Quarantine Threat (incident updated)** — Same as above, but triggered on incident update.

3. **Isolate Workload (incident created)** — Triggers on **critical severity + malicious verdict** → stops process, quarantines, **isolates the workload**, adds comment, sends email notification.

4. **Isolate Workload (incident updated)** — Triggers when positivity level > 9 → same isolation actions.

5. **Malware Incident Requiring Attention** — Triggers when high/critical malware incidents remain uninvestigated for **8+ hours** → stops process, quarantines, sends email.

6. **Incident Requiring Attention** — Triggers when high/critical incidents remain uninvestigated for **24+ hours** → stops process, quarantines, sends email.

---

### Network Isolation Options

When isolating a workload (either via workflow or manually from the cyber kill chain), you can choose from these **immediate actions after isolation**:

- **Isolate only**
- **Isolate and backup workload**
- **Isolate and backup workload with forensic data**
- **Isolate and power off workload**

You can also configure **network exclusions** to allow specific ports, URLs, hostnames, and IP addresses to remain accessible during isolation (with direction control: incoming and outgoing, incoming only, or outgoing only).

> **Important:** Even when a workload is isolated, all **Acronis Cyber Protect technologies remain functional**, so you can still conduct a full investigation.

### Reconnecting an Isolated Workload

To reconnect, you can either:
- **Connect to network immediately**
- **Recover workload from backup before connecting to network**

---

### Configuring an EDR Workflow

1. In the Cyber Protect console, go to **Management > Workflows**.
2. Click the ellipsis icon (**...**) next to the workflow and select **Open**.
3. Modify **Condition** blocks (**All** = all conditions must be met; **Any** = at least one).
4. Modify **Action** blocks as needed (e.g., change email recipients).
5. Click **Save and enable** to activate, or **Save** to keep it in Draft/Disabled state.

question: What are the possible states of a workflow version, and which state allows editing?
ideal_answer: Workflow versions can be in three states: Draft, Active, or Disabled. Only versions in the Draft state can be edited. eval_rag_answer:
A workflow version can be in one of three states: **Draft**, **Active**, or **Disabled**.

Only versions in the **Draft** state can be edited. A Draft version is one that was not previously activated on any tenant. Once a version becomes Active (enabled for at least one tenant), it can only be set to Disabled, and a Disabled version was previously enabled but is currently inactive — neither can be edited.

chunk_title: Acronis XDR Query Language (XQL) and Sample Queries
section: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
question: How do I filter EDR events by date using XQL in Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud?
ideal_answer: Use the `ago` function in a XQL `where` clause to filter by date. The `ago` function accepts a numeric value followed by a time unit: days (d), hours (h), minutes (m), or seconds (s). For example, `eventType | where column < ago(3d)` filters for events older than 3 days, and `column < ago(30s)` filters for events older than 30 seconds.
eval_rag_answer:
In **Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud**, you can filter EDR events by date in XQL using two methods:

---

## Method 1: The `ago` function (relative date filtering)

The **`ago`** function filters events relative to the current time. It accepts a numeric value followed by a time unit:

| Time Unit | Abbreviation | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Days | `d` | `ago(3d)` — 3 days ago |
| Hours | `h` | `ago(3h)` — 3 hours ago |
| Minutes | `m` | `ago(3m)` — 3 minutes ago |
| Seconds | `s` | `ago(30s)` — 30 seconds ago |

**Syntax:**

```
eventType | where column < ago(3d)
```

**Examples:**

```
WinProcCreate | where event_time < ago(3d)
```
↑ Returns process creation events older than 3 days.

```
WinNetAccess | where event_time < ago(12h)
```
↑ Returns network access events older than 12 hours.

---

## Method 2: Exact DateTime value (absolute date filtering)

You can also filter using an exact date in **`YYYY-MM-DD`** format, surrounded by single or double quotes:

```
WinProcCreate | where event_time < '2022-11-01'
```
↑ Returns process creation events before November 1, 2022.

---

## Key DateTime fields available for filtering

Most EDR event types include these DateTime fields you can filter on:

- **`event_time`** — When the event occurred
- **`timestamp`** — Event timestamp
- **`proc_start`** — Process start time
- **`parent_start`** — Parent process start time
- **`login_time`** / **`logout_time`** — For `WinLogin` / `WinLogout` events

## Combining with other filters

You can combine date filters with other conditions using **AND** / **OR**:

```
WinProcCreate | where event_time < ago(7d) AND host_name == 'Server01'
```

---

**Note:** Events search is accessed from **Protection > Events search** in the Cyber Protect console. The default retention period for EDR event search results is **seven days**.

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/05-xql-query-language-and-sample-queries.md`, `10-edr-xdr/06-event-types-and-fields.md`

Core ago() function explanation is perfectly accurate.

chunk_title: Acronis XDR Query Language (XQL) and Sample Queries
section: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
question: What is the difference between CONTAINS and ICONTAINS operators in Acronis XQL, and how does case sensitivity work by default?
ideal_answer: In XQL, CONTAINS performs case-sensitive partial string matching, while ICONTAINS performs case-insensitive partial string matching. Column searches are case sensitive by default. Additionally, CONTAINS can match against a single value or a list of values using the syntax `CONTAINS ('String1', 'String2', 'String3')`. Both operators also have negated versions: NOT CONTAINS and NOT ICONTAINS.
eval_rag_answer:
In **Acronis XQL (Extended Query Language)**, the difference between **CONTAINS** and **ICONTAINS** is as follows:

| Operator | Case Sensitivity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **`CONTAINS`** | **Case-sensitive** | Performs partial string matching respecting letter case |
| **`ICONTAINS`** | **Case-insensitive** | Performs partial string matching ignoring letter case |

### Key Details:

1. **Default behavior:** Column searches in XQL are **case-sensitive by default**.
2. **`CONTAINS`** can match against a single value or a list of values using the syntax:
   ```
   CONTAINS ('String1', 'String2', 'String3')
   ```
3. Both operators have **negated versions**:
   - **`NOT CONTAINS`** — excludes case-sensitive matches
   - **`NOT ICONTAINS`** — excludes case-insensitive matches

### Example Usage:

- `field CONTAINS 'Error'` — matches "Error" but **not** "error" or "ERROR"
- `field ICONTAINS 'Error'` — matches "Error", "error", "ERROR", etc.

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/05-xql-query-language-and-sample-queries.md`


`WinProcCreate` is the event type for Windows process creation events. You pipe it with the `where` keyword to filter by the `host_name` column using the `==` operator for exact string matching. question: What data types are supported for Boolean values in EDR XQL queries, and how can they be represented?
ideal_answer: Boolean values in XQL queries can be represented in two ways: as integers (1 for true, 0 for false) or as literal keywords (true or false). For example, both `WinLogin | where is_admin == 1` and `WinLogin | where is_admin == true` are valid expressions.
eval_rag_answer:
In **EDR XQL queries**, Boolean values can be represented in **two ways**:

1. **As integers:**
   - `1` for **true**
   - `0` for **false**

2. **As literal keywords:**
   - `true`
   - `false`

Both representations are valid and interchangeable. For example, the following two queries are equivalent:

```
WinLogin | where is_admin == 1
```

```
WinLogin | where is_admin == true
```

section: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
question: How do I remediate an entire incident in Acronis EDR?
ideal_answer: In the Cyber Protect console, go to Protection > Incidents, click the investigate icon for the incident to display the cyber kill chain, then click 'Remediate entire incident'. Select an analyst verdict (True positive or False positive), then follow the guided remediation steps: Stop threats, Quarantine threats, and Rollback changes (for true positives) or define prevention actions (for false positives).
eval_rag_answer:
Here's how to **remediate an entire incident** in **Acronis EDR**:

## Steps to Remediate an Entire Incident

1. In the **Cyber Protect console**, go to **Protection > Incidents**.
2. Click the **investigate icon** for the incident. The **cyber kill chain** is displayed.
3. Click **Remediate entire incident**. The Remediate entire incident dialog is displayed.
4. In the **Analyst verdict** section, select one of the following:
   - **True positive** — Select if you are sure the attack is a legitimate attack. You then add remediation and prevention actions.
   - **False positive** — Select if you are sure the attack is not a genuine attack. You can only define prevention actions in this mode.

---

### If True Positive — Remediation Actions

The following steps must be performed **in sequential order**:

1. **Step 1 — Stop threats**: Select to stop all processes related to the threat.
2. **Step 2 — Quarantine threats**: Once the threat is stopped, select to quarantine all malicious and suspicious processes and files.
3. **Step 3 — Rollback changes**: After threats have been quarantined, select to delete any new registry entries, scheduled tasks, or files created by the threat (and any of its children threats). The rollback process then reverts any modifications made by the threat to the registry, scheduled tasks, and/or files existing on the workload prior to the attack.
   - The rollback process tries to recover items from the **local cache** first. Items that fail to recover will be recovered from **backup images**.
   - Select **Allow this response action to access encrypted backups using your stored credentials** if access to the relevant backups is encrypted.
   - Click **Affected items** to view all items (files, registry, or scheduled tasks) affected by the rollback, the actions applied (Delete, Recover, or None), and the recovery source.
4. **Recover workload** (if previous steps fail completely or partially):
   - **Recover workload from backup** — Recover from a specific recovery point.
   - **Disaster recovery failover** — Run disaster recovery if enabled in your protection plan. Recommended for critical workloads such as AD servers or database servers.

### Prevention Actions (Available for Both True and False Positive)

- **Add to blocklist** (True positive): Select and choose the relevant protection plan list to block all detections of the incident from being executed.
- **Add to allowlist** (False positive): Select the relevant protection plans to prevent the incident from being detected again.
- **Patch workload**: Select to patch any vulnerable software. Choose restart behavior: **Do not restart**, **Always restart**, or **Restart if required**.

### Finalize the Remediation

5. Select the **Change investigation state of the incident to: Closed** check box (if not selected, the investigation state remains in its previous state).
6. Add a **Comment** with details about the remediation.
7. Click **Remediate**. The remediation actions are executed step by step. Once clicked, the button displays **Go to activities** to review all response actions.

---

### If False Positive

1. In the **Analyst verdict** section, select **False positive**.
2. In the **Prevention actions** section, select **Add to allowlist** and choose the relevant protection plans.
3. Select **Change investigation state of the incident to: False positive**.
4. Click **Remediate**.

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/09-remediating-incidents.md`
eval_issues: RAG answer matches ideal answer perfectly with additional useful detail about each step.

section: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
question: What happens differently when you select 'False positive' versus 'True positive' as the analyst verdict during EDR incident remediation?
ideal_answer: When you select 'True positive', you can add both remediation and prevention actions, including stopping threats, quarantining them, and rolling back changes. When you select 'False positive', you can only define prevention actions (such as adding the incident to a protection plan allowlist) — remediation actions like stopping or quarantining threats are not available.
eval_rag_answer:
Great question! The documentation clearly explains the key differences between selecting **False positive** and **True positive** as the analyst verdict during EDR incident remediation.

## True Positive

Select **True positive** when you are sure the attack is a **legitimate attack**. This unlocks both **remediation and prevention actions**:

### Remediation Actions (sequential order):
1. **Step 1 – Stop threats**: Stop all processes related to the threat.
2. **Step 2 – Quarantine threats**: Quarantine all malicious and suspicious processes and files.
3. **Step 3 – Rollback changes**: Delete any new registry entries, scheduled tasks, or files created by the threat (and its children), then revert any modifications made to the registry, scheduled tasks, and/or files that existed prior to the attack. You can optionally select **Allow this response action to access encrypted backups using your stored credentials** and click **Affected items** to review all items impacted by the rollback.
4. **Recover workload**: If any remediation steps fail (completely or partially), choose from:
   - **Recover workload from backup** (select a specific recovery point)
   - **Disaster recovery failover** (recommended for critical workloads like AD servers or database servers)

### Prevention Actions:
- **Add to blocklist**: Blocks all detections of the incident from executing for selected protection plans.
- **Patch workload**: Patches vulnerable software with configurable restart options.

---

## False Positive

Select **False positive** when you are sure the attack is **not a genuine attack**. In this mode:

- You can **only define prevention actions** — no remediation actions are available.
- The prevention action is **Add to allowlist** (not blocklist): select the relevant protection plans to prevent the incident from being **detected** again.
- The investigation state checkbox changes to **Change investigation state of the incident to: False positive**.

---

## Key Differences Summary

| Aspect | True Positive | False Positive |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| **Meaning** | Legitimate attack confirmed | Not a genuine attack |
| **Remediation actions** | ✅ Available (Stop, Quarantine, Rollback, Recover) | ❌ Not available |
| **Prevention action** | **Add to blocklist** (block execution) | **Add to allowlist** (prevent future detection) |
| **Investigation state** | Changed to **Closed** | Changed to **False positive** |

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/09-remediating-incidents.md`
eval_issues: RAG answer is accurate and detailed but includes extra details (rollback specifics, recover workload, patch workload) not in the ideal answer. It doesn't explicitly mention the False positive prevention action of adding to a protection plan allowlist - instead mentions 'Add to blocklist'. Minor: doesn't clearly state that False positive only allows prevention actions (no remediation).

section: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
question: Which types of cyber kill chain nodes can be added to a blocklist or allowlist in Acronis EDR?
ideal_answer: The blocklist and allowlist option is available for Process, File, and Network cyber kill chain nodes. When adding to a blocklist, you must also select the relevant protection plan(s) the action should apply to. You can later remove a node from the blocklist or allowlist by following the same steps and clicking Remove instead of Add.
eval_rag_answer:
In **Acronis EDR**, the following cyber kill chain node types can be added to a **blocklist** or **allowlist**:

1. **Process** — Processes involved in the attack
2. **File** — Files impacted by the attack
3. **Network** — Network connections

## How to Add to Blocklist or Allowlist

### Add to Blocklist
1. In the cyber kill chain, click the **process**, **file**, or **network** node you want to remediate.
2. In the displayed sidebar, click the **Response Actions** tab.
3. In the **Prevent** section, click the arrow icon next to **Blocklist**.
4. Select the relevant protection plan(s) you want to apply this action to.
5. (Optional) Add a comment.
6. Click **Add**. The process, file, or network will be prevented from launching in the future.

### Add to Allowlist
1. In the cyber kill chain, click the **process**, **file**, or **network** node you want to remediate.
2. In the displayed sidebar, click the **Response Actions** tab.
3. In the **Prevent** section, click the arrow icon next to **Allowlist**.
4. Select the relevant protection plan(s) you want to apply this action to.
5. (Optional) Add a comment.
6. Click **Add**. The process, file, or network will be excluded from future detections.

> **Note:** To remove a node from a blocklist or allowlist later, follow the same steps but click **Remove** instead of **Add**.

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/11-response-actions-files-registry-blocklist-allowlist.md`

chunk_title: Automated Workflows, Monitoring Mode, and Testing EDR
section: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
question: What are the specific trigger conditions that cause the 'Isolate Workload' workflow to activate when an EDR incident is updated versus when it is created?
ideal_answer: When an EDR incident is created, the Isolate Workload workflow triggers when: Threat status is 'Not mitigated', Incident state is 'Not started', Severity is 'Critical', and Verdict is 'Malicious'. When an EDR incident is updated, the conditions differ: Threat status must be 'Not mitigated', Positivity Level must be greater than 9, and Incident type must be 'Process detected' or 'Malware detected'. The updated trigger notably uses Positivity Level > 9 instead of Severity and Verdict conditions.
eval_rag_answer:
Great question! The documentation clearly defines the different trigger conditions for the two **Isolate Workload** workflows. Here are the specific differences:

---

## Isolate Workload (when EDR incident is **created**)

- **Trigger:** EDR incident is created
- **Conditions (ALL must be met):**
  1. **Threat status** = `Not mitigated`
  2. **Incident state** = `Not started`
  3. **Severity** = `Critical`
  4. **Verdict** = `Malicious`

- **Actions:** Stop process → Quarantine process → Isolate workload → Add comment → Send email

---

## Isolate Workload (when EDR incident is **updated**)

- **Trigger:** EDR incident is updated
- **Conditions (ALL must be met):**
  1. **Threat status** = `Not mitigated`
  2. **Positivity Level** > `9`
  3. **Incident type** = `Process detected` **OR** `Malware detected`

- **Actions:** Same as above (Stop process → Quarantine process → Isolate workload → Add comment → Send email)

---

## Key Differences

| Aspect | On Created | On Updated |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **Severity check** | `Critical` required | Not required |
| **Verdict check** | `Malicious` required | Not required |
| **Incident state** | `Not started` required | Not required |
| **Positivity Level** | Not checked | Must be **> 9** |
| **Incident type** | Not checked | Must be `Process detected` or `Malware detected` |

The **"on created"** variant focuses on **severity and verdict** (Critical + Malicious), while the **"on updated"** variant instead relies on a **high positivity level (> 9)** combined with specific **incident types**. This design ensures that even incidents that weren't initially flagged as Critical/Malicious can trigger workload isolation if they escalate to a very high confidence of being a true attack upon update.

Both workflows can be customized via **Management > Workflows** in the Cyber Protect console by modifying conditions and actions in the visual flowchart editor.

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/12-automated-workflows-monitoring-testing.md`
eval_issues: None. The RAG answer perfectly matches the ideal answer with all trigger conditions correctly specified, and provides additional helpful context like the comparison table and action sequences.

chunk_title: Working with the XDR Graph, Investigating Nodes, and Response Actions
section: Extended Detection and Response (XDR)
question: How does the XDR graph display malicious files found inside compressed archives in the FortiMail Workspace Security integration?
ideal_answer: When a malicious file was extracted from a zip archive attachment or other type of compressed archive, a separate file node is created for the zip archive, and a line connects the malicious file node to the attachment node. This differs from direct email attachments, where the malicious file node connects directly to the email node.
eval_rag_answer:
Here is how the **XDR graph** displays malicious files found inside compressed archives in the **FortiMail Workspace Security** integration:

## FortiMail Workspace Security — Compressed Archive Display in XDR Graph

When a malicious file is extracted from a **zip archive** (or other type of compressed archive) that was an email attachment, the XDR graph displays it as follows:

1. **A file node is created for the zip/compressed archive attachment** — this represents the original archive that was attached to the email.
2. **A line connects the malicious file node to the attachment (archive) node** — showing the relationship that the malicious file was contained within the compressed archive.

This is distinct from the simpler case where a malicious file is a **direct attachment** of an email, in which case a line connects the malicious file node directly to the **email node**.

### Summary of FortiMail XDR Graph Connection Patterns

| Scenario | Graph Representation |
|----------|---------------------|
| Malicious file is a **direct email attachment** | Line connects malicious file node → email node |
| Malicious file **extracted from a compressed archive** | A file node is created for the archive; line connects malicious file node → archive attachment node |
| Malicious **URL** in an email | Line connects malicious URL node → email node |

### How to Investigate

1. In the **Cyber Protect console**, go to **Protection > Incidents**.
2. Click the **investigate icon** for the relevant incident.
3. Click the **XDR** tab.
4. Navigate to the relevant node and click it to display the sidebar.
5. Review the **Overview** tab for file details, and the **Response actions** tab for available actions (e.g., **blacklist sender** for FortiMail).

> **Note:** The email node for FortiMail Workspace Security integration is labeled with the **sender's email address**, and includes details about the sender's IP address, name, client used, and the name, format, and size of each attachment.

**Source:** `10-edr-xdr/14-xdr-graph-and-response-actions.md`
eval_issues: Minor: added extra details (URL scenario, investigation steps) not in ideal answer but not incorrect. Core answer perfectly matches ideal.

section: Acronis Managed Detection and Response (MDR)
question: What are the differences between Standard and Advanced MDR service levels in Acronis?
ideal_answer: Standard MDR provides 24x7 monitoring, automated mitigation and response, incident analysis with remediation recommendations, and basic monthly reports, but leaves remediation actions under partner control. Advanced MDR includes all Standard features plus manual advanced mitigation and response, root cause analysis with incident reports, threat intelligence, advanced monthly reports, proactive threat hunting, and on-demand incident investigation.
eval_rag_answer:
## Standard vs Advanced MDR

**Standard MDR** provides 24/7 monitoring and expert investigation, but leaves remediation actions under partner control — analysts confirm incidents and provide detailed remediation *recommendations* only.

**Advanced MDR** is a fully managed service where Acronis analysts actively *execute* mitigation and response actions themselves. It also includes **proactive monthly threat hunting** to identify advanced/stealthy threats that don't trigger standard alerts, and delivers **advanced reporting** beyond the standard monthly operational reports.

In short: Standard = monitor + advise; Advanced = monitor + act + hunt.

section: Acronis Managed Detection and Response (MDR)
question: How many escalation contacts can be configured for MDR, and what happens during an escalation if no one answers?
ideal_answer: You can configure up to 4 escalation contacts in total. During an escalation, Acronis TRU follows the escalation path and calls each contact up to six times before moving to the next contact. After a call or if no contact is made, Acronis TRU sends an email to all contacts with an overview of the escalation and the incident.
eval_rag_answer:
You can configure up to **4 escalation contacts** in total for the **Acronis MDR** service.

Here's what happens during an escalation if no one answers:

1. **Acronis TRU** (Threat Research Unit) follows the configured escalation path.
2. Each contact is called **up to 6 times** before moving to the next contact in the list.
3. After a call is made — or if **no contact is reached** — Acronis TRU sends an **email to all contacts** with an overview of the escalation and the incident.

This ensures that even if phone contact fails, all escalation contacts are still notified via email about the security incident.

Here's how to connect your **Microsoft CSP account** to **Acronis Cyber Protect Cloud** for Microsoft 365 management:

> **Note:** This feature is only available for **Partner administrator** users.

## Connecting to a Microsoft CSP Account

1. In the **Cyber Protect console**, go to **M365 management > Configuration**.
2. Click **Connect Microsoft account**. You will be redirected to the Microsoft login page.
3. Sign in to the Microsoft account with your **CSP credentials**, and accept the permissions requested by Acronis. The permissions are for the **Octiga Multi-Tenant Security** enterprise application registered in Entra ID, connected with the service provider's Microsoft 365 tenant.
4. In the displayed **Customer mapping** tab, map the Microsoft 365 clients with the relevant customers in Cyber Protect Cloud.

When you log in to your Microsoft CSP account, the system **automatically retrieves all customer tenants** associated with the CSP account.

## Customer Mapping

After onboarding is completed, map the tenants to your customers:

1. Go to **Microsoft 365 management > Configuration**.
2. In the **Customer mapping** tab, select the relevant Microsoft 365 tenant(s), then click **Map to existing customer**.
3. Select the relevant customer from the dropdown and click **Map**.

When successfully mapped:
- **Mapped** status is shown in the **Mapping status** column.
- The system applies the predefined **tenant baselines** to the customer tenant.
- **Security posture monitoring** starts and runs automatically for the customer tenant.

## Licensing

- Under **service-based licensing**, this is part of the paid offering **RMM - Microsoft 365 seats**.
- Under **solution-based licensing**, it is included in the **Security and RMM** and the **Ultimate Protection** license, under the Microsoft 365 seats offering.
- A charge is applied for each user allocated with an **Exchange Online mailbox license**.

> **Tip:** When onboarding tenants with a large number of users (over 1000), the system might take some time to retrieve all details, as each user needs to be verified and some Microsoft APIs can only be triggered once every two seconds.

**Source:** `12-rmm/02-m365-environment-management.md`
